channel.title: Home on Alan Aw
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Alan Aw

item.title: Review of “Who We Are and How We Got Here"
item.description: One of my goals this year is to read more books and to think critically about them. I’ve started a spreadsheet tracking books I’ve read and books I’m reading, and I’m excited to share it here. I just finished reading David Reich’s book on ancient DNA (aDNA) and human history, Reich, David. Who We Are and How We Got Here: Ancient DNA and the new science of the human past.

item.title: Lagrange Multipliers Revisited
item.description: Happy New Year! This is my first post, and I intend to use this space to write about my thoughts and learning. Any constructive feedback would be appreciated! Now for the actual post. I’ve been thinking about mathematics over Winter break, mostly because I often find myself overlooking details about important results and consequently learning maths not as thoroughly as I’d like. I find this especially true with regard to analysis (or continuous maths in general), so I try to revisit the maths every now and then.

item.title: A Plain Markdown Post
item.description: This sample post is mainly for blogdown users. If you do not use blogdown, you can skip the first section. 1. Markdown or R Markdown This is a post written in plain Markdown (*.md) instead of R Markdown (*.Rmd). The major differences are: You cannot run any R code in a plain Markdown document, whereas in an R Markdown document, you can embed R code chunks (```{r}); A plain Markdown post is rendered through Blackfriday, and an R Markdown document is compiled by rmarkdown and Pandoc.

item.title: Hello R Markdown
item.description: R Markdown This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com. You can embed an R code chunk like this: summary(cars) ## speed dist ## Min. : 4.0 Min. : 2.00 ## 1st Qu.:12.0 1st Qu.: 26.00 ## Median :15.0 Median : 36.00 ## Mean :15.4 Mean : 42.98 ## 3rd Qu.

About Me code{white-space: pre;} pre:not([class]) { background-color: white; } if (window.hljs) { hljs.configure({languages: []}); hljs.initHighlightingOnLoad(); if (document.readyState && document.readyState === "complete") { window.setTimeout(function() { hljs.initHighlighting(); }, 0); } } h1 { font-size: 34px; } h1.title { font-size: 38px; } h2 { font-size: 30px; } h3 { font-size: 24px; } h4 { font-size: 18px; } h5 { font-size: 16px; } h6 { font-size: 12px; } .

item.title: About Me
item.description: I am a first-year PhD student in the Department of Statistics at the University of California at Berkeley. I am broadly interested in statistical modeling, statistical theory, and their applications to biology and the social sciences. I completed my undergraduate studies in Mathematical and Computational Science (with a Biology minor) at Stanford University, where I was advised by Bradley Efron. I was fortunate to do research in mathematical and computational biology with Noah Rosenberg, Marcus Feldman and Julia Palacios.

item.title: Research
item.description: Human Evolution “Raiding at Monte Alban.” Source: First Civilizations, PBS What do we know about our past? The study of human history is, and continues to be, transformed by the availability of genome data. The collection of new genomes along with their high-depth sequencing allow statistical models of evolution to be implemented at larger scales to reveal insights into how human populations migrated, admixed, or experienced population decline. Uncovering these evolutionary phenomena of our past enables us to learn more precisely about how we came to be, and the conclusions can be corrobarated with or even challenge incumbent theories about human societies.

item.title: Stat 135 (Spring 2019)
item.description: I serve as a GSI for this upper division undergraduate course providing a first introduction to mathematical statistics. Based on problems provided by the Instructor Adam Lucas, I prepared and used slides to facilitate team work during section. I also occasionally wrote notes for students to supplement my in-class teaching. Here is a thematic summary I typed up for students to strategize for their final exam review. Week 1: Introduction Week 2: Sampling, parametric models / R setup and practice Week 3: MoM estimation / bootstrap practice [slides] Week 4: Maximum likelihood estimation, CRLB [slides] Week 5: Sufficiency [slides] Week 6: Hypothesis testing, Neyman-Pearson lemma, duality of HT and CI [slides] [notes] Week 7: Comparing two samples: parametric Week 8: (Midterm review) [jeopardy slides] Week 9: Likelihood ratio test, UMP test, GLRT [slides] Week 10: (Spring break) Week 11: Comparing two samples: non-parametric, chi-square tests Week 12: ANOVA Week 13: Linear model (single predictor), least squares [notes] Week 14: Linear model (multiple predictors) Week 15: Bayesian statistics [slides]

item.title: Teaching
item.description: Teaching Philosophy I recently wrote a statement of teaching philosophy as part of a statistics teaching seminar, which you can read here. Look below for courses I have taught and feel free to click around for material. Current Stat 135: Concepts of Statistics (Instructor: Adam Lucas) [material]
